<title level="1" right="Rules">[Removing Afflictions](/Rules.aspx?ID=2399)</title>

**Source** [Player Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=216) pg. 431

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
Apart from waiting them out, afflictions can be removed through certain uses of the skills and spells. The [Treat Disease](/Actions.aspx?ID=2397) and [Treat Poison](/Actions.aspx?ID=2398) uses of [Medicine](/Skills.aspx?ID=42) are commonly used to treat those afflictions.

The [_cleanse affliction_](/Spells.aspx?ID=1467) spell is also available to most spellcasters. Spells that counteract conditions at the source, such as [_sound body_](/Spells.aspx?ID=1679), can also be effective against diseases and poisons that cause those conditions.

Curses are trickier, requiring solutions that specifically mention them, such as a 4th-rank _cleanse affliction_ or the [Break Curse](/Feats.aspx?ID=5128) skill feat.
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

markdown:
**Source** [Player Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=216) pg. 432 The ways a creature can use Perception depend on what senses it has. The primary concepts you need to know for understanding senses are precise senses, imprecise senses, and the three states of detection a target can be in: [observed](/Rules.aspx?ID=2415), [hidden](/Rules.aspx?ID=2416), or [undetected](/Rules.aspx?ID=2417). Vision, hearing, and scent are three prominent senses, but each has a different degree of acuity.
Detecting with Other Senses Most abilities that designate “a creature you can see” or the like function just as well if the user can precisely sense the subject with a different sense. If a monster uses a sense other than vision, the GM can adapt ways of avoiding detection that work with the monster's senses. For example, a creature that has echolocation might use hearing as a primary sense. This could mean its quarry is [concealed](/Rules.aspx?ID=2419) in a noisy chamber, [hidden](/Rules.aspx?ID=2416) in a great enough din, or [invisible](/Rules.aspx?ID=2420) under a [_silence_](/Spells.aspx?ID=1674) spell.
Using Stealth With Other SensesThe [Stealth](/Skills.aspx?ID=48) skill is designed to use [Hide](/Actions.aspx?ID=2404) for avoiding visual detection and [Avoid Notice](/Actions.aspx?ID=2622) and [Sneak](/Actions.aspx?ID=2405) to avoid being both seen and heard. For many special senses, a player can describe how they're avoiding detection by that special sense and use the most applicable Stealth action. For instance, a creature stepping lightly to avoid being detected via [tremorsense](/Rules.aspx?ID=2413) would be using Sneak.
In some cases, rolling a Dexterity-based Stealth skill check to Sneak doesn't make the most sense. For example, a PC trying to avoid being detected by a creature that senses heartbeats might meditate to slow their heart rate, using Wisdom instead of Dexterity for their Stealth check. When a creature could detect you using multiple different senses, use your lowest applicable attribute modifier.

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Imprecise Senses](/Rules.aspx?ID=2407)</title>

**Source** [Player Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=216) pg. 433

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
Average hearing is an imprecise sense—it can’t detect the full range of detail that a precise sense can. You can usually sense a creature automatically with an imprecise sense, but it has the [hidden](/Conditions.aspx?ID=79) condition instead of the [observed](/Conditions.aspx?ID=84) condition. It might be [undetected](/Rules.aspx?ID=2417) by you if it’s using [Stealth](/Skills.aspx?ID=48) or is in an environment that distorts the sense, such as a noisy room in the case of hearing. In those cases, you have to use the [Seek](/Actions.aspx?ID=2301) basic action to detect the creature. At best, an imprecise sense can be used to make an undetected creature (or one you didn’t even know was there) merely hidden—it can’t make the creature observed.
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Vague Senses](/Rules.aspx?ID=2408)</title>

**Source** [Player Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=216) pg. 433

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
A character also has many vague senses—ones that can alert you that something is there but aren't useful for zeroing in on it to determine exactly what it is. The most useful of these for a typical character is the sense of smell. At best, a vague sense can be used to detect the presence of an [unnoticed](/Rules.aspx?ID=2418) creature, making it [undetected](/Rules.aspx?ID=2417). Even then, the vague sense isn't sufficient to make the creature [hidden](/Rules.aspx?ID=2416) or [observed](/Rules.aspx?ID=2415).

When one creature might detect another, the GM almost always uses the most precise sense available.

Pathfinder's rules assume that a given creature has vision as its only precise sense and hearing as its only imprecise sense. Some characters and creatures, however, have precise or imprecise senses that don't match this assumption. For instance, a character with poor vision might treat that sense as imprecise, an animal with the [scent](/MonsterAbilities.aspx?ID=74) ability can use its sense of smell as an imprecise sense, and a creature with echolocation or a similar ability can use hearing as a precise sense. Such senses are often given special names and appear as “echolocation (precise),” “scent (imprecise) 30 feet,” etc.
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Special Senses](/Rules.aspx?ID=2409)</title>

**Source** [Player Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=216) pg. 433

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
While a [human](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=64) might have a difficult time making creatures out in [dim light](/Rules.aspx?ID=2403), an [elf](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=60) can see those creatures just fine. And though elves have no problem seeing on a moonlit night, their vision can’t penetrate complete [darkness](/Rules.aspx?ID=2404), whereas a [dwarf’s](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=59) can. Special senses allow a creature to ignore or reduce the effects of the [undetected](/Rules.aspx?ID=2417), [hidden](/Rules.aspx?ID=2416), or [concealed](/Rules.aspx?ID=2419) conditions when it comes to situations that foil average vision.
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Darkvision and Greater Darkvision](/Rules.aspx?ID=2410)</title>

**Source** [Player Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=216) pg. 433

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
A creature with darkvision or greater darkvision can see perfectly well in areas of [darkness](/Rules.aspx?ID=2404) and [dim light](/Rules.aspx?ID=2403), though such vision is in black and white only. Some forms of magical darkness, such as a 4th-rank [_darkness_](/Spells.aspx?ID=1480) spell, block normal darkvision. A creature with greater darkvision, however, can see through even these forms of magical darkness.
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Scent](/Rules.aspx?ID=2412)</title>

**Source** [Player Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=216) pg. 433

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
Scent involves sensing creatures or objects by smell, and is usually a vague sense. The range is listed in the ability, and it functions only if the creature or object being detected emits an aroma. If a creature emits a heavy aroma or is upwind, the GM can double or even triple the range of scent abilities used to detect that creature, and the GM can reduce the range if a creature is downwind.
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Tremorsense](/Rules.aspx?ID=2413)</title>

**Source** [Player Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=216) pg. 433

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
Tremorsense allows a creature to feel the vibrations through a solid surface caused by movement. It is usually an imprecise sense with a limited range (listed in the ability). Tremorsense functions only if the detecting creature is on the same surface as the subject, and only if the subject is moving along (or [burrowing](/Actions.aspx?ID=2310) through) the surface.
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Detecting Creatures](/Rules.aspx?ID=2414)</title>

**Source** [Player Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=216) pg. 434

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
Three conditions measure the degree to which you can sense a creature: [observed](/Conditions.aspx?ID=84), [hidden](/Conditions.aspx?ID=79), and [undetected](/Conditions.aspx?ID=96). However, the [concealed](/Conditions.aspx?ID=62) and [invisible](/Conditions.aspx?ID=83) conditions can partially mask a creature, and the [unnoticed](/Conditions.aspx?ID=98) condition indicates you have no idea a creature is around.

With the exception of invisible, these conditions are relative to the viewer—it's possible for a creature to be observed to you but hidden from your ally. Most of these rules apply to objects as well as creatures.

Typically, the GM tracks how well creatures detect each other, since neither party has perfect information. For example, you might think a creature is in the last place you sensed it, but it was able to [Sneak](/Actions.aspx?ID=2405) away. Or you might think a creature can't see you in the dark, but it has [darkvision](/Rules.aspx?ID=2410).

You can attempt to avoid detection by using the [Stealth](/Skills.aspx?ID=48) skill to [Avoid Notice](/Actions.aspx?ID=2622), [Hide](/Actions.aspx?ID=2404), or [Sneak](/Actions.aspx?ID=2405), or by using [Deception](/Skills.aspx?ID=38) to [Create a Diversion](/Actions.aspx?ID=2387).
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Observed](/Rules.aspx?ID=2415)</title>

**Source** [Player Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=216) pg. 434

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
In most circumstances, you can sense creatures without difficulty and target them normally. Creatures in this state are [observed](/Conditions.aspx?ID=84). Observing requires a [precise sense](/Rules.aspx?ID=2406), which for most creatures means sight. If you can’t observe the creature, it’s either [hidden](/Conditions.aspx?ID=79), [undetected](/Conditions.aspx?ID=96), or [unnoticed](/Conditions.aspx?ID=98), and you’ll need to factor in the targeting restrictions. Even if a creature is observed, it might still be [concealed](/Conditions.aspx?ID=62).
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Hidden](/Rules.aspx?ID=2416)</title>

**Source** [Player Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=216) pg. 434

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
A creature that's [hidden](/Conditions.aspx?ID=79) is only barely perceptible. You know what space it occupies, but little else. Perhaps the creature just moved behind [cover](/Rules.aspx?ID=2372) and successfully used the [Hide](/Actions.aspx?ID=2404) action. Your target might be behind a waterfall, where you can see some movement but can't determine an exact location. Maybe you've been [blinded](/Conditions.aspx?ID=59) or the creature is [invisible](/Conditions.aspx?ID=83), but you used the [Seek](/Actions.aspx?ID=2301) basic action to determine its general location based on hearing alone. Regardless of the specifics, you're [off-guard](/Conditions.aspx?ID=58) to a hidden creature.

When targeting a hidden creature, before you roll to determine your effect, you must attempt a DC 11 flat check. If you fail, you don't affect the creature, though the actions you used are still expended—as well as any spell slots, costs, and other resources. You remain off-guard to the creature, whether you successfully target it or not.
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Undetected](/Rules.aspx?ID=2417)</title>

**Source** [Player Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=216) pg. 434

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
If a creature is [undetected](/Conditions.aspx?ID=96), you don't know what space it occupies, you're [off-guard](/Conditions.aspx?ID=58) to it, and you can't easily target it. Using the [Seek](/Actions.aspx?ID=2301) basic action can help you find an undetected creature, usually making it [hidden](/Conditions.aspx?ID=79) from you instead of undetected. If a creature is undetected, that doesn't necessarily mean you're unaware of its presence—you might suspect an undetected creature is in the room with you, even though you're unable to find its space. The [unnoticed](/Conditions.aspx?ID=98) condition covers creatures you're unaware of.

Targeting an undetected creature is difficult. If you suspect there's a creature around, you can pick a square and attempt an attack. This works like targeting a hidden creature, but the flat check and attack roll are both rolled in secret by the GM. The GM won't tell you why you missed—whether it was due to failing the flat check, rolling an insufficient attack roll, or choosing the wrong square. The GM might allow you to try targeting an undetected creature with some spells or other abilities in a similar fashion. Undetected creatures are subject to area effects normally.

For instance, suppose an enemy [elf](/Ancestries.aspx?ID=60) [wizard](/Classes.aspx?ID=39) cast [_invisibility_](/Spells.aspx?ID=1577) and then [Sneaked](/Actions.aspx?ID=2405) away. You suspect that with the elf's Speed of 30 feet, they probably moved 15 feet toward an open door. You move up and attack a space 15 feet from where the elf started and directly on the path to the door. The GM secretly rolls an attack roll and flat check, but they know that you were not quite correct—the elf was actually in the adjacent space! The GM tells you that you missed, so you decide to make your next attack on the adjacent space, just in case. This time, it's the right space, and the GM's secret attack roll and flat check both succeed, so you hit!
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Concealed](/Rules.aspx?ID=2419)</title>

**Source** [Player Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=216) pg. 434

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
A [concealed](/Conditions.aspx?ID=62) creature is in mist, within [dim light](/Rules.aspx?ID=2403), or amid something else that obscures sight but isn’t a physical barrier. When you target a creature that’s concealed from you, you must attempt a DC 5 flat check before you roll to determine your effect. If you fail, you don’t affect the target. The concealed condition doesn’t change which of the main categories of detection apply. A creature in a light fog bank is still observed even though it’s concealed.
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

markdown:
<title level="1" right="Rules">[Invisible](/Rules.aspx?ID=2420)</title>

**Source** [Player Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=216) pg. 434

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
A creature with the [invisible](/Conditions.aspx?ID=83) condition (by way of an [_invisibility_](/Spells.aspx?ID=1577) spell, for example) is automatically [undetected](/Conditions.aspx?ID=96) to any creatures relying on sight as their only [precise sense](/Rules.aspx?ID=2406). Precise senses other than sight ignore the invisible condition. You can [Seek](/Actions.aspx?ID=2301) to attempt to figure out an invisible creature's location, making it only hidden from you. This lasts until the invisible creature successfully uses [Sneak](/Actions.aspx?ID=2405) to become undetected again. If you're already [observing](/Conditions.aspx?ID=84) a creature when it becomes invisible, it starts out [hidden](/Conditions.aspx?ID=79), since you know where it was, though it can then Sneak to become undetected.

Other effects might partially foil invisibility. For instance, if you were tracking an invisible creature's footprints through the snow, the footprints would make it hidden. Throwing a [net](/Equipment.aspx?ID=2738) over an invisible creature would make it observed but [concealed](/Conditions.aspx?ID=62) for as long as the net is on it.
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>

<title level="1" right="Rules">[Encounter Mode](/Rules.aspx?ID=2421)</title>

**Source** [Player Core](/Sources.aspx?ID=216) pg. 435

<row gap="medium">
<column gap="medium" flex="1 1 400px">
When every individual action counts, you enter the encounter mode of play. In this mode, time is divided into rounds, each of which is 6 seconds of time in the game world. Every round, each participant takes a turn in an established order. During your turn, you can use actions, and depending on the details of the encounter, you might have the opportunity to use reactions and free actions on your own turn and on others’ turns.
</column>

<column gap="medium">

</column>

</row>
